bestWhen: Group is meeting in this configuration for the first time or context has shifted since last session.
avoidWhen: Group already shares strong context and a long intro will drain energy.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Move from facilitator framing to peer-to-peer warm-up so voices activate before deeper work.
Once context is set, immediately clarify shared purpose before content work begins.
Pair framing with a short impulse to give the group a common reference point.

useInstead.reason: Skip facilitator-led framing; let participants surface their own context through paired conversation.

profiles.notes: Keep under 10 minutes; longer intros signal to participants that this is a presentation, not a workshop.

bestWhen: Group needs the same baseline information before diverging or making sense of a topic.
avoidWhen: Information could be pre-read or when the group already holds the relevant expertise.

pairsWellAfter.reason: After framing, deliver the substantive impulse the rest of the workshop will work on.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Process the input immediately so the group converts passive listening into shared sensemaking.
Use input as a provocation, then let participants explore implications in depth.
Use the input to expose paradoxes the group must hold rather than resolve.

useInstead.reason: Distribute expertise across multiple voices instead of one expert speaking to all.
Same knowledge transfer but framed as a live conversation, which keeps the group engaged.

profiles.notes: Keep input blocks under 10 minutes; if longer is needed, split into chunks separated by short processing structures.

bestWhen: A dense or activating module just ended and the group needs to integrate before moving on.
avoidWhen: Energy is already low and the group needs activation, not more inward work.

pairsWellAfter.reason: A quick reflection helps cluster and name what emerged.
After open exploration, reflection lets each participant land their own takeaway.
After surfacing stories of success, give people a quiet moment to extract personal meaning.
Reflection helps participants distill what they took away from rotating exposure to peers.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Move from individual reflection to a collective debrief and decision about next steps.
Reflection surfaces personal insight; 15% Solutions converts it into a concrete next step.

useInstead.reason: Guided journaling produces a richer individual artifact than a generic reflection prompt.
Provides explicit phases for sensemaking and action instead of open-ended reflection.

profiles.notes: Specify the prompt; vague 'just reflect' instructions produce vague output. Solo first, then optional pair share.

bestWhen: You need every voice to engage a question and the loudest voices tend to dominate.
avoidWhen: The question is trivial or the group is too small to benefit from progressive grouping.

pairsWellAfter.reason: Process an input immediately so listening converts to engagement instead of fading.
Once the room is warmed up, channel the energy into a structured generative round.
After purpose is clear, use 1-2-4-All to generate ideas grounded in that purpose.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Move from generating ideas to prioritizing them across the whole group.
Convert collective insight into individual concrete next actions.
After surfacing ideas, deepen the most promising ones with consultation rounds.

useInstead.reason: Many short rotating prompts surface breadth quickly when 1-2-4-All would feel slow.
Use when the group needs deeper dialogue rather than rapid synthesis.

profiles.notes: Always commit to the timing (1 / 2 / 4 / all) up front; cutting the solo phase guts the inclusion benefit.

bestWhen: Group needs to break a problem-fixated frame and recover what already works.
avoidWhen: A real crisis is unaddressed; forced positivity will feel dismissive.

pairsWellAfter.reason: Once context is set, surfacing success stories early builds energy and trust.
After warm-up, deeper paired storytelling lands more easily.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Use 1-2-4-All to extract patterns and conditions for success from the stories shared.
Process the stories into shared insights and decisions about what to amplify.
Success patterns can inform concrete future designs.

useInstead.reason: Single deep interview is faster than many paired rounds when time is tight.
Less prescriptive; useful when the topic is more sensemaking than success mining.

profiles.notes: Build in time for the pattern-extraction step; just sharing stories without harvest leaves value on the table.

bestWhen: Group is stuck adding more solutions and needs to see what to stop doing first.
avoidWhen: Climate is already cynical; TRIZ can amplify negativity without a constructive turn.

pairsWellAfter.reason: Clarifies the core purpose before attempting to systematically destroy it.
After framing the challenge, TRIZ inverts it productively to surface counterproductive habits.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Convert the 'stop doing' list into individual next actions within personal control.
Helps define the few rules that prevent backsliding.
Move from stop-list to a portfolio view of what to release across all activities.

useInstead.reason: Maps the full lifecycle of activities, including release, when a single stop-list is too narrow.
Surfaces paradoxes without moving as directly into stop-doing behavior.

profiles.notes: Run all three steps; without step 3 (which of these are we actually doing?) the structure becomes a venting exercise.

bestWhen: Distinct functions or teams must work together and unspoken needs are blocking progress.
avoidWhen: Trust is too low to handle public requests; or roles are not actually interdependent.

pairsWellAfter.reason: Shared purpose makes cross-functional requests land as collaboration, not blame.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each role takes its accepted requests into concrete actions within personal control.
Use the agreements surfaced to define the minimum cross-functional rules going forward.

useInstead.reason: Builds the helping muscle peer-to-peer without the public role-on-role exposure of WINFY.
Gives practical help with lower exposure than direct cross-functional requests.

profiles.notes: Strict timing matters; WINFY only works if requests stay short and yes/no/whatever rules are kept clean.

bestWhen: A shared experience or working session needs structured debrief into next steps.
avoidWhen: There is no shared experience to look back on; the structure has nothing to anchor to.

pairsWellAfter.reason: Provides structure after open dialogue on a complex issue.
Synthesizes insights across rotating exposures into a single set of actions.
Field observations are useless without structured sensemaking; this provides it.
After viewing artifacts, this debrief converts impressions into shared insight and action.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Now What ideas land better when each person commits to a step within their control.
When Now What surfaces many candidate actions, prioritize them across the whole group.

useInstead.reason: Better when individuals need more private processing time first.
Lighter alternative when there is no time or need for the full three-phase debrief.

profiles.notes: Keep the three phases visibly separate; merging What and So What collapses observation into interpretation.

bestWhen: Group is rushing to action without shared clarity on why the work matters.
avoidWhen: Purpose is already crisp and shared; the structure will feel patronizing.

pairsWellAfter.reason: Warm-up creates the relational ground for honest answers about purpose.
Right after framing, surface deep purpose before any content work begins.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Take the deep purpose surfaced and turn it into principles, participants, structure, practices.
Once purpose is clear, design how the work will actually unfold.

useInstead.reason: Goes beyond purpose into the supporting elements; use when time and stakes warrant.
Surfaces paradoxes around purpose rather than driving toward a single statement.

profiles.notes: Pairs are essential; the structure relies on a partner pushing the 'why' question patiently without leading.

bestWhen: Group is mixed or new and you need rapid activation and voice equality.
avoidWhen: Group is small (<8) where the rotation feels artificial, or trust is so low people freeze.

pairsWellAfter.reason: Move from facilitator framing to peer activation; voices are warm before deeper work.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Channel the activated energy directly into structured idea generation.
Move from many short exchanges into longer, deeper sensemaking on the same theme.
Warm-up makes deeper paired storytelling easier; people are already in conversation mode.
Once people are warmed up, partner work on purpose lands more honestly.

useInstead.reason: Several short prompts in rotation when one open question feels too vague to anchor people.

profiles.notes: Question quality matters; a vague 'introduce yourself' prompt wastes the structure. Tie it to the workshop topic.

bestWhen: A profound or ambiguous challenge needs slow, equal-voice exploration before any decisions.
avoidWhen: Time is tight or the group is in execution mode; the slow rounds will feel indulgent.

pairsWellAfter.reason: After a provoking impulse, give the group time to make sense of it together at depth.
Move from short paired exchanges into a longer, anchored exploration of the same theme.
When the workshop centers on a single hard topic, go deep early after framing.
After surfacing paradoxes, Conversation Cafe gives time to sit with them productively.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Convert open exploration into shared insight and concrete next steps.
Land the deep conversation in personal next steps within each participant's control.
When the cafe surfaces many candidate directions, prioritize them across the whole room.

useInstead.reason: Faster, more structured surfacing when the topic does not warrant a long sit.
Better when content is anchored to specific projects or artifacts, not one open question.

profiles.notes: Talking object and explicit agreements are not optional; without them the structure collapses into normal group talk.

bestWhen: Closing a workshop with concrete next steps inside each person's scope
Group risks staying abstract after analysis or ideation
avoidWhen: Group needs a single shared decision instead of individual commitments
Participants lack real authority or resources to act on their own

pairsWellAfter.reason: Top crowd-sourced ideas become individual commitments inside each person's authority.
Birth and renewal opportunities turn into concrete personal moves people can start now.
After sorting challenges, people commit to small moves on simple and complicated work.
Once paradoxical tensions are named, each person finds a 15% step they can take inside them.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each 15% step gets immediate peer help to sharpen the first move.
Short solo crystallisation locks in the chosen first action before the room disperses.
Debrief the commitments together and surface what becomes possible next.

useInstead.reason: Use when each person needs help with a stuck challenge, not just to commit to next steps.

profiles.notes: Strong default closer. Keep prompts tight: 'What can you do without new permission, budget, or resources?'

bestWhen: Need to surface and rank bold ideas with a whole group of 12+ quickly
Want anonymous filtering to let unconventional ideas rise
avoidWhen: Group is smaller than ~12 (sifting loses signal)
Topic is deeply personal or political and needs slower sense-making

pairsWellAfter.reason: After the purpose is sharp, ideas score against the real 'why' of the work.
A warmed-up room produces stronger first-cut ideas before scoring.
A short framing impulse calibrates what counts as a bold idea.
Paradoxes generate richer, more provocative bold ideas to sift.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Top-scored ideas become individual commitments people can act on today.
Top ideas become inputs to plan a productive sequence of next moves.
Owners of top ideas get peer help on the first concrete move.

useInstead.reason: Use for groups under ~12 or when ranking matters less than building shared ideas.
Use when you need shared sense-making rather than ranked actionable ideas.

profiles.notes: Needs index cards or a remote tool that supports anonymous 0-5 scoring across five rounds.

bestWhen: Group needs a fast, energising context shift with many quick paired exchanges
Opening a session where people barely know each other but the topic is shared
avoidWhen: Very low trust where prompts would feel intrusive
Pure remote without breakout pairs and clear rotation choreography

pairsWellAfter.reason: A warm framing makes the rapid prompts land instead of feeling like a quiz.
A short impulse gives the prompts a shared reference point.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Themes that surface in the rapid pairs get deepened and refined.
Warmed-up room produces stronger bold ideas to sift.
Energised group is ready to slow down and make sense together.

useInstead.reason: Use when you want focused 2-min rounds on the same question rather than rotating prompts.

profiles.notes: Prompt design carries this. Keep prompts open, future-leaning, and non-judgmental; rotate every 60-90 seconds.

bestWhen: Need quiet integration after intense input or shared experience
Group is over-talking and individuals haven't caught up with their own thinking
avoidWhen: Group needs immediate collective synthesis or external dialogue
Participants are strongly averse to writing or working solo

pairsWellAfter.reason: After hearing success stories, individuals sift personal patterns and questions.
Lets a dense impulse settle before group conversation starts.
Quiet processing turns multiple short exposures into personal insight.
Field observations need solo time to crystallise into patterns.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Solo insights become richer pair and group conversation.
Open questions from the journal become exactly the right peer-help topic.
Personal insights translate directly into a small first action.

useInstead.reason: Use when you only need a brief crystallisation, not a deeper layered exploration.
Use when you want sharpened thinking via a fixed question sequence rather than open spiral exploration.

profiles.notes: Provide a printed template with concentric prompts; works equally well in a shared doc remotely.

bestWhen: Want individual focus and sharp answers before group dialogue
Topic benefits from progressive narrowing through sequenced prompts
avoidWhen: Group needs immediate live exchange or has strong writing aversion
Question is too vague to design a meaningful sequence

pairsWellAfter.reason: A short framing gives the writing sequence its shared starting point.
Field notes get sharpened into patterns through structured writing rounds.
Paradoxes become productive when each person writes through them in stages.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Sharpened individual answers feed strong pair and group conversation.
Final statements posted on the wall become a shared landscape to walk through.
Final-round writing surfaces the exact challenge to bring to peers.

useInstead.reason: Use when conversation will produce better thinking than solo writing.
Use when you want open layered exploration rather than a tightening question sequence.

profiles.notes: Design 3-5 progressively sharpening prompts; 2-3 minutes per round usually beats long writing windows.

bestWhen: Group is over-verbal and needs a non-rational route into the topic
Integrating complex personal or collective experience that resists words
avoidWhen: Low trust or strong 'I can't draw' resistance with no permission-setting time
Tight schedule with no space to debrief what the drawings reveal

pairsWellAfter.reason: After sharing success stories, drawing surfaces patterns words missed.
A dense impulse gets digested visually before any group talk.
Paradoxes often resolve into images more easily than into sentences.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Pairs interpret each other's drawings, then groups build shared themes.
Drawings become the gallery; the walk turns private images into shared meaning.
Visuals seed a slower sense-making conversation about what they reveal.

useInstead.reason: Use when group resists drawing but still needs quiet personal sense-making.

profiles.notes: Set permission early: 'stick figures count'. Always pair with a structure that interprets the drawings.

bestWhen: Need to make diversity of opinion on a charged question visible and bodily
Group has been talking abstractly and needs to take a position
avoidWhen: Strong hierarchy in the room makes public positioning risky for juniors
Fully remote setting where physical placement can't be replicated

pairsWellAfter.reason: A framing impulse defines the spectrum's poles clearly enough to position on.
A warm opening earns the safety needed to take a public position.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Visible disagreement becomes the agenda for slower shared sense-making.
Mixed-position pairs work the question further before group synthesis.
Visible polarity is the raw material for naming a productive paradox.

useInstead.reason: Use when slow shared sense-making matters more than making positions visible.
Use when the topic is too sensitive for public positioning but still needs fast divergence.

profiles.notes: Prepare 2-4 sharp either/or statements. Always invite folding so opposite views meet face-to-face after positioning.

bestWhen: Group is stuck in either/or thinking on a complex strategic challenge
Need to surface productive tensions before planning or deciding
avoidWhen: Group needs a simple direct answer rather than to sit with paradox
Low context on the challenge so paradoxes would be invented, not discovered

pairsWellAfter.reason: Portfolio tensions across phases become sharp paradoxical questions.
Once purpose is clear, the real paradoxes the group must hold become visible.
Complex-quadrant work names the paradoxes that sorting alone can't resolve.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Groups work each paradox to find moves that hold both sides.
Named paradoxes get worked into both-and operating choices.
Each person finds a small move they can take inside the paradox today.

useInstead.reason: Use when you need to sort challenges by type rather than name productive paradoxes.
Use when you need to resolve a known polarity into both-and moves, not surface new ones.

profiles.notes: Quality of the paradoxes matters more than quantity. Two strong wicked questions usually beat six weak ones.

bestWhen: Experienced group with high trust working on a personal change or stuck pattern
Need to surface inner blockers that abstract analysis can't reach
avoidWhen: Mixed seniority, performance context, or any setting where inner work could be weaponised
No time or follow-up structure to hold what surfaces

pairsWellAfter.reason: Empathy practice primes the group to handle personal disclosure with care.
A warm, careful opening sets the safety this structure needs.
A short impulse on inner blockers normalises naming them.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Insight about a blocker turns into the smallest possible move against it.
Each named demon becomes a precise help-request for peers.
A gentle solo close lets what surfaced settle before the next module.

useInstead.reason: Use when you want a clearer protocol for working with inner constraints in pairs.
Use when trust is too low for naming personal blockers aloud but reflection is still needed.

profiles.notes: Brief participants explicitly that sharing depth is optional. Never use as an opener or with strangers.

bestWhen: Before launching a change initiative where unspoken fears would otherwise sabotage it
Group senses something is off but no one has named the worries out loud
avoidWhen: High political tension where naming fears could be weaponised
No follow-up structure to take what surfaces seriously

pairsWellAfter.reason: A short framing of the coming change focuses what worries are about.
A warm opening earns the safety to put fears into shared view.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Surfaced fears feed directly into 'how would we make it worse?' for productive release.
Each big worry becomes a help-request that gets practical peer input.
Each person finds a small move that addresses one of their named worries.
Group integrates what was named and decides what changes in the plan.

useInstead.reason: Use when you'd rather invert the fears into 'worst possible outcome' play than name them directly.
Use when surfacing tensions as paradoxes feels safer than naming fears in the open.

profiles.notes: Pre-commit visibly to what will happen with the worries that surface; without that, this structure damages trust.

bestWhen: The group treats different challenge types with the same approach
You need to triage a mixed portfolio of problems before choosing methods
avoidWhen: Only one well-defined challenge is on the table
There is no shared list of issues to sort

pairsWellAfter.reason: A short framing of the four domains lands better than diving in cold.
Sort the surfaced challenges by agreement and certainty to choose appropriate next methods.
Clarifying purpose first sharpens which challenges actually matter to sort.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Items in the simple and complicated zones are ready for concrete personal action.
Items landing in the complex zone benefit from articulating the underlying paradoxes.
Complicated items need the step-by-step planning that storyboards provide.

useInstead.reason: If the question is portfolio life-cycle rather than challenge-typing, ecocycle gives a richer view at higher cost.

profiles.notes: Prepare a visible 2x2 (agreement vs certainty) and a stack of ~10 candidate challenges; sort them together rather than lecturing the model.

bestWhen: The team is stretched across too many initiatives
There is rigidity in legacy work or scarcity in new growth
Leadership wants to decide what to stop, not only what to start
avoidWhen: The portfolio is tiny or already obvious
Group has no shared knowledge of the current activities

pairsWellAfter.reason: A 5-minute framing of the eight ecocycle phases prevents confusion at the board.
Use the surfaced ideas as the inventory you then place on the ecocycle.
A sharpened purpose makes positioning activities on the cycle far less arbitrary.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Activities in renewal can be acted on immediately with each person's 15%.
Items flagged for creative destruction set up stop-doing work via TRIZ.
After deciding what to keep, define minimal guardrails for it.

useInstead.reason: If you only need to triage problems by type rather than review a whole portfolio life-cycle.

profiles.notes: Build the ecocycle figure visibly large; let people self-place activities before facilitating discussion of clusters.

bestWhen: Innovation keeps stalling at a different level than where it was created
Cross-scale dynamics matter: team, org, market, regulation
avoidWhen: Group is unfamiliar with systems thinking and time is short
The challenge is concrete and operational, not multi-scale

pairsWellAfter.reason: Ecocycle warms the brain for life-cycle thinking before adding nested levels with panarchy.
A short framing of nested adaptive cycles is essential before the exercise lands.
A network map gives panarchy concrete actors to place at each system level.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Panarchy insights become useful when each level translates to a specific personal action.
Cross-scale dynamics often expose paradoxes worth articulating explicitly.

useInstead.reason: If a single-level life-cycle view is enough, ecocycle is faster and more accessible than nested panarchy.

profiles.notes: Prepare large visuals of the three nested cycles; this is heavy cognitive lifting, not a quick warmup.

bestWhen: A cross-functional working group is stuck and relationships seem to be the bottleneck
You suspect either too much sameness or too little reason to collaborate
avoidWhen: Active toxic conflict needs repair work first
Relationships are not the actual constraint on the work

pairsWellAfter.reason: Light connection across the room warms people up for an honest STAR rating of their working relationships.
A clear shared purpose sets the bar for what generative relationships are for.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each person commits to a small move that improves one of the four STAR dimensions.
Once patterns are visible, WINFY converts insights into concrete asks and offers across functions.
Use peer consulting on the specific relationship patterns each person wants to shift.

useInstead.reason: If you need concrete needs and commitments now, WINFY skips the diagnostic and goes to requests.
Cards give a richer pattern language but take longer than the four STAR dimensions.

profiles.notes: Score the four STAR dimensions privately first; share scores before discussing why they differ.

bestWhen: A team is redesigning how it interacts and a richer pattern language helps
You want to compare current and aspirational collaboration patterns
avoidWhen: You do not have a card set or remote substitute prepared
Time pressure demands concrete actions over pattern naming

pairsWellAfter.reason: STAR surfaces the symptom; the cards offer a richer pattern vocabulary to explain it.
A short framing of pattern thinking helps participants use the cards as a language, not a quiz.
Real observed behaviors give the cards something concrete to attach to.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each person picks one pattern shift they personally have authority to try.
Translate chosen patterns into a tight set of must-dos and must-not-dos for the team.

useInstead.reason: STAR's four dimensions cover the main relational patterns faster, without the card set.
If you want to map who connects to whom and act on it, webbing is more direct.

profiles.notes: Have physical cards or a digital deck ready; pre-select a working subset rather than dumping the full library on the table.

bestWhen: Assumptions about users or operations feel stale or contested
Design or policy decisions need grounding in actual behavior
avoidWhen: You cannot get safe, ethical access to the field
The workshop window is too short to send people out and come back

pairsWellAfter.reason: A short briefing on observation discipline keeps notes descriptive, not interpretive.
Clarifying purpose tells observers what to actually pay attention to.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Small groups present field findings in rotating stations so everyone hears them.
Structured debrief turns raw field notes into shared meaning and next steps.
Posting field notes for a silent walk-through reveals patterns across observations.

useInstead.reason: If interviewing people is enough and field access is not feasible, appreciative interviews are faster.
If experienced practitioners are in the room, a fishbowl surfaces practice knowledge without going out.

profiles.notes: Send observers in pairs with a tight shared lens; separate description from interpretation in the notes template.

bestWhen: Rules and process have sprawled and the team needs guardrails, not micromanagement
Before scaling or handing off an initiative to others
avoidWhen: The purpose itself is unclear - clarify it first
Group still needs to diverge on options before constraining them

pairsWellAfter.reason: A sharp purpose is the anchor against which max specs are tested for being truly necessary.
Use the prioritized ideas as the candidate list of must-dos and must-not-dos to challenge.
After designing a step-by-step plan, distill it down to the essential specs.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Within tight specs, each person identifies what they can act on now.
Peers help each person sharpen how they will work within the specs.

useInstead.reason: If you need movement now rather than codified guardrails, 15% goes straight to personal action.

profiles.notes: Generate a fat list of max specs first, then ruthlessly cut anything not strictly required to achieve the purpose.

bestWhen: The group is stuck in either/or debates such as standardize vs autonomy or local vs global
A polarity needs ongoing balancing, not a one-time pick
avoidWhen: A clean either/or decision is genuinely required
No real polarity exists - the choice is just hard

pairsWellAfter.reason: Wicked questions surface the underlying paradox so integrated~autonomy can work it concretely.
Shared purpose anchors which elements truly need to be standardized vs left autonomous.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Lock the both/and balance into a tight set of must-dos and must-not-dos.
Each person identifies one move that holds both poles in their own work.

useInstead.reason: If naming the paradox is enough and you do not yet need to design both/and elements, wicked questions is faster.

profiles.notes: List candidate elements, then sort each into 'standardize globally' or 'leave to local autonomy' with explicit reasons.

bestWhen: Participants bring real, current challenges they want help with
Mixed expertise can produce surprising perspectives
Time is short and you want everyone helped in parallel
avoidWhen: People have no real challenge to bring
Trust is too low to expose a struggle to two peers
Group is stuck in advice-giving habits and will not slow down

pairsWellAfter.reason: Practices listening and helping skills first, raising the quality of advice in the triads.
Builds quick connection and warms people up to share real challenges in small triads.
Frames the purpose and the kind of challenges to bring.
Helps participants sharpen the why behind their challenge before asking for help.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Turns advice from triads into concrete next steps within each person's freedom and resources.
Lets participants distill what was actually useful from the advice received.
Group debrief surfacing patterns across all triads' challenges and advice.

useInstead.reason: Single client gets the full group's wisdom instead of two peers; deeper but slower per person.
Scales the wise-crowds shape to many participants when the group is too big for clean triads.

profiles.notes: Works very well in remote breakouts; clear timing and roles are essential.

bestWhen: One or two people have a meaty challenge worth the group's full attention
Group has diverse expertise the client can benefit from
Client is willing to listen silently while peers consult
avoidWhen: Many people need help simultaneously (use Troika instead)
Group is too large to give one client real airtime
Trust is too low to expose a meaningful challenge to all

pairsWellAfter.reason: Sharpens the client's challenge framing so the group's advice lands on the real question.
Primes the group to ask before advising, improving the quality of the consult.
Builds enough connection for a single client to expose a real challenge.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Helps the client convert wide-ranging advice into immediately actionable next steps.
Gives the client space to integrate what mattered most from the consult.
Group reflects on patterns observed across multiple client consults.

useInstead.reason: Everyone gets to be a client in parallel triads instead of one client at a time.
Same logic adapted for groups too big for the original format.

profiles.notes: Strict role discipline (client listens silently) is what makes this work; brief carefully.

bestWhen: Large group with multiple challenge holders who all need real airtime
Enough facilitators or strong self-organization to run parallel rounds
There is genuine cross-functional expertise to draw on
avoidWhen: Group is small enough for the original Wise Crowds
Pure remote without strong breakout coordination
Time is too tight to run multiple full rounds

pairsWellAfter.reason: Each client sharpens their own challenge before the parallel consults begin.
Aligns many helpers across many groups on a shared way of helping.
Quickly mixes a large group so consult tables are not just same-team clusters.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each client converts the advice from their table into a concrete next step.
Whole-group debrief surfaces patterns across all the parallel consults.
Quiet space for clients to integrate what they heard.

useInstead.reason: Smaller, simpler version when the group is not actually large enough to need scaling.
Everyone is a client in parallel triads; faster and lighter for very large groups.

profiles.notes: Coordinate parallel rounds with crisp timing; appoint table hosts.

bestWhen: About to run peer consultation structures (Troika, Wise Crowds, DAD)
Team wants to build a shared culture of asking for and giving help
People default to advice giving and need a reset
avoidWhen: Group is already fluent in peer help and the practice would feel basic
No follow-on activity that puts the new skills to use
Time pressure makes a skill-practice exercise feel like overhead

pairsWellAfter.reason: Pairs people once they are warm and connected enough to practice honestly.
Frames why helping skills matter for the rest of the session.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Lets participants apply the helping behaviors they just practiced on real challenges.
Improves the quality of consultative listening before a single-client format.
Sharpens the asking and listening posture used throughout DAD's seven questions.
Deepens listening from helping skills into pure empathic presence.

profiles.notes: Treat as a primer; its value compounds when followed by a real consultation structure.

bestWhen: Building empathy before difficult conversations or conflict surfacing
Team formation moments where people want to be known more fully
Inclusion or culture work where listening is the main muscle to grow
avoidWhen: Trust is so low that personal stories feel unsafe
There is no time for a careful closing after stories surface
The group expects fast pace and concrete output, not reflection

pairsWellAfter.reason: Sets a tone of care so the listening practice feels intentional, not awkward.
Warms up connection before pairing for deeper personal stories.
Briefly frames why deeper listening matters for what follows.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Quiet integration of what surfaced in the listening pairs.
Carries the listening posture into a wider sense-making conversation.
Channels improved listening into peer consultation on real challenges.
Names patterns and implications from the stories that surfaced.

profiles.notes: Brief listeners explicitly: no advice, no comparison, no fixing.

bestWhen: Real loss, layoff, ending, or profound change is alive in the group
Sufficient trust and an experienced facilitator are present
Time and space allow a careful closing, not a hard stop
avoidWhen: There is no genuine grief context; using it as an exercise will feel exploitative
Trust is low or participation is not fully consensual
Schedule cannot hold the emotional aftermath
Facilitator is not experienced with grief work

pairsWellAfter.reason: Carefully frames purpose, consent, and what participants can expect.
Builds the listening capacity needed before walking through loss together.
Names the loss explicitly and gives the practice a respectful container.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Gentle, quiet integration; no rush to action after grief work.

useInstead.reason: Acknowledgment and empathy without explicitly naming grief; appropriate when readiness is unclear.

profiles.notes: Requires informed consent, an experienced facilitator, and protected closing time.

bestWhen: Launching or unsticking an initiative that depends on informal allies
Group knows the players but has not yet seen the whole pattern
There is a clear purpose the network should serve
avoidWhen: No clear purpose to map relationships against
Group lacks knowledge of the relevant stakeholders
Mapping would surface political risk the group is not ready for

pairsWellAfter.reason: Locks in the purpose the network is being mapped to serve.
Provides the strategic context that shapes whose connections matter.
Frames the why and the boundaries of the mapping exercise.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each person picks concrete next steps to strengthen specific connections in the map.
Plans the engagement sequence with the people the map highlights.
Quiet integration of what the map revealed before committing to actions.
Surfaces what the group needs from the key people the map identified.

useInstead.reason: Analyzes the quality of existing relationships rather than mapping the broader network.

profiles.notes: Use a digital whiteboard remote; prepare stakeholder categories ahead of time.

bestWhen: A chronic problem keeps recurring and the group shares the frustration
You want bottom-up local solutions rather than top-down fixes
Positive deviants likely exist within the group itself
avoidWhen: Problem is brand new and no one has lived experience yet
Decision context is strictly top-down and ideas have nowhere to land
Group cannot stay with the seven questions long enough

pairsWellAfter.reason: Warms people up to share lived experience honestly with peers.
Briefly grounds the problem context before opening it up.
Frames the chronic problem and the spirit of the seven-question dialogue.
Sharpens why the chronic problem matters before searching for local solutions.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Converts emerging local solutions into immediate, owned next steps.
Prioritizes the most powerful ideas surfaced across the dialogue.
Personal integration before any commitment is made.
Group sense-making on patterns and implications across the dialogue.

useInstead.reason: Builds on root causes of past success rather than working a chronic problem.

profiles.notes: The seven questions are the structure; do not improvise them away.

bestWhen: People are stuck on a goal and limiting beliefs may be the real blocker
Before action planning when assumptions need to be tested
Coaching contexts where naming inner constraints helps movement
avoidWhen: Trust is too low to voice a limiting belief out loud
There is no real stuck situation and the exercise will feel forced
Large group without breakout structure for the inner dialogue

pairsWellAfter.reason: Surfaces what each person feels they could do, exposing where blocks really sit.
Briefly explains why limiting beliefs matter for the work to come.
Sets a reflective frame so participants take the inner work seriously.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Once a limiting belief is loosened, name what becomes possible right now.
Quiet integration after surfacing personal assumptions.
Bring the now-named belief to peers for help acting beyond it.
Translates surfaced beliefs into shared patterns and actions.

useInstead.reason: Lighter inner-blocker format with a similar intent and quicker run time.
Names counterproductive behaviors to stop rather than surfacing inner beliefs.

profiles.notes: Pairs or triads work best; remote requires breakouts with clear timing.

bestWhen: A leader or expert has critical experience the group needs to absorb
You want to humanize leadership and surface real stories before group work
avoidWhen: The interviewee is rehearsed and won't speak candidly
The group needs to act and produce, not listen

pairsWellAfter.reason: Warms up the room and surfaces participants' questions before the interview begins.
Short framing sets context and the role of the celebrity before they take the stage.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Lets participants make sense of what they heard and translate insights into next steps.
Structured debrief turning the interview's stories into shared meaning and next moves.
Converts the inspiration from the interview into concrete personal next actions.

useInstead.reason: Several practitioners share live in the inner circle instead of one staged interview.
Multiple innovators host stations rather than concentrating attention on a single voice.

profiles.notes: Brief the interviewer carefully; the questions decide whether this lands as candid sharing or PR.

bestWhen: Groups have produced posters, flipcharts, or boards others should absorb
You want movement and quiet absorption between two intense modules
avoidWhen: There are no prior artifacts to walk through
Group is too large to circulate without crowding stations

pairsWellAfter.reason: Storyboards displayed as a gallery let groups compare designs before merging or choosing.
Drawings are perfect gallery material; participants absorb each others' visual stories.
Outputs from breakouts can be posted, then absorbed by everyone via a walk.
Top ideas can be posted on the wall and walked through to deepen understanding.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Structured reflection on what was seen across the gallery turns observations into actions.
Surfaces patterns and questions noticed during the walk before the next module.
Personal next steps inspired by what each person noticed during the walk.

useInstead.reason: Presenters host stations and explain artifacts live, instead of silent absorption.

profiles.notes: In remote format, use a Miro/Mural board with timed silent exploration before group reflection.

bestWhen: Five to seven innovators have working examples worth spreading
You want connections between innovators and curious peers
avoidWhen: No clear presenters with concrete things to share
Group is small enough to share in plenary

pairsWellAfter.reason: Warms the room and surfaces what people most want to learn before they choose stations.
Short framing of the theme and station logistics before rotations begin.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each participant takes one new idea from the stations and turns it into a personal next step.
Plenary debrief surfacing patterns across stations and what to act on next.
Sift the most actionable ideas spread across stations before deciding where to invest.

useInstead.reason: Silent absorption of artifacts without dedicated presenters; faster but less interactive.
Concentrate attention on one expert if you don't have multiple innovators to host stations.
Single shared focus on practitioners in the inner circle, no rotation logistics.

profiles.notes: Plan rotation timing tightly (2-3 rounds of 10-15 min); presenters get exhausted faster than expected.

bestWhen: Several practitioners can share field experience with a wider community
You want listening and absorption before broad group work
avoidWhen: Inner circle isn't truly experienced or is just senior by title
Group needs to act and decide, not absorb

pairsWellAfter.reason: Audience surfaces their burning questions before the inner circle starts.
Brief framing of the topic and the inner circle members before they begin.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Outer circle processes insights into questions and follow-up actions.
Structured reflection on what the practitioners shared and what to take forward.
Each listener identifies one practice from the inner circle they can try this week.

useInstead.reason: Single interviewee with a moderator, simpler to facilitate when only one expert is available.
Everyone speaks at small tables instead of listening to a designated inner circle.
Distribute attention across multiple innovators at stations rather than one inner circle.

profiles.notes: Brief inner-circle members on candor expectations; rehearsed answers kill the format's value.

bestWhen: A diverse group must self-organize around a complex shared theme
Sponsors genuinely want emergent direction, not pre-decided outcomes
avoidWhen: The outcome is already decided and OST is being used as theatre
Group lacks authority or willingness to act on what surfaces

pairsWellAfter.reason: Surfaces participants' real questions and energy before they post sessions.
Sharpens the OST theme so the marketplace gets purposeful sessions, not random topics.
Names the paradoxes the group must hold while self-organizing the marketplace.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each session leader and participant names what they will personally do next week.
Whole-group sense-making across the many session reports before closing.
Sifts the strongest proposals across all session reports for collective focus.

useInstead.reason: Surface and prioritize ideas in 30 minutes when there's no time for full self-organization.
Predefined stations with set hosts when an emergent agenda would feel too risky.

profiles.notes: The four principles and Law of Two Feet must be introduced and held; skipping this collapses the format.

bestWhen: Facing high uncertainty and high stakes where one strategy isn't enough
Leadership needs to stress-test plans against plausible futures
avoidWhen: The future is reasonably predictable for the decision horizon
Group lacks authority or context to pick uncertainties that matter

pairsWellAfter.reason: Names the paradoxes that often become the two axes of the uncertainty matrix.
Sorting challenges by certainty surfaces which ones deserve scenario work.
Portfolio view of current activities sharpens which uncertainties most threaten them.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Translates 'survival rules' from each scenario into the minimum specs the strategy must hold.
Each leader picks one robust action that holds up across scenarios and starts now.
Group makes sense of the four scenarios and decides which signals to monitor.

useInstead.reason: Faster way to sort issues by certainty when full scenario planning would be too heavy.
Whole-portfolio review without scenario-building when uncertainties aren't the bottleneck.

profiles.notes: Picking the two axes is the hard part; validate they're truly uncertain and high-impact before building scenarios.

bestWhen: You need to design a meeting or session collaboratively, not solo
LS structures must be sequenced toward a clear meeting outcome
avoidWhen: Purpose isn't clarified yet (run Nine Whys first)
Designers don't know enough LS structures to choose well

pairsWellAfter.reason: Locks the purpose so the storyboard targets a real outcome, not a generic agenda.
Brief input on the LS palette gives less-experienced designers building blocks to use.
Constraints on what the meeting must and must not do shape the storyboard's structure choice.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Compare storyboards side-by-side to merge strengths or pick the strongest design.
Sharpen and pressure-test the storyboard's flow before locking it.
Each designer commits to a first concrete step they'll take to prepare the meeting.

useInstead.reason: Use when scope is a transformation initiative rather than a single meeting.
Faster commitment to next steps when full session design isn't needed.

profiles.notes: Have a printed LS menu visible; the storyboard quality depends on what structures designers actually know.

bestWhen: Designing a multi-week or multi-month transformation initiative
A senior team needs a concrete sequenced plan to commit to
avoidWhen: Scope is a single meeting (use Design StoryBoards instead)
Initiative purpose and principles aren't clarified yet

pairsWellAfter.reason: P2P locks the five elements; advanced storyboards then sequence the actual rollout.
Portfolio view shows what to start, sustain, or release before designing the sequence.
Hard constraints from min-specs anchor what the storyboard must respect.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each owner names a concrete first step within their authority before leaving the room.
Sense-making and adjustment after the first phase of the storyboard runs in the field.

useInstead.reason: Use the standard variant when the scope is a single meeting, not a project.
When the initiative needs foundational design (purpose, principles) more than sequencing.

profiles.notes: Pair with clear ownership per phase; without owners the sequenced plan becomes a wish list.

bestWhen: Launching a new initiative that needs resilient foundations
Restarting or refounding an existing initiative that has drifted
avoidWhen: A quick decision is enough; foundational design would be overkill
Authority to set purpose and principles is missing in the room

pairsWellAfter.reason: Sharpens the deeper purpose before P2P's first element locks it for the initiative.
Reveals what to release or sustain so the new initiative isn't competing with old work.
Names paradoxes the initiative must hold; useful before drafting principles.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each participant commits to a personal first move grounded in the new principles.
Once the five elements are set, storyboards sequence the rollout step by step.
Convert the principles into hard must-do/must-not-do rules for daily practice.
Periodic reviews of how the five elements are holding up in practice.

useInstead.reason: When the initiative needs sequenced execution more than foundational design.
Sharpens purpose alone in 20 minutes when full P2P is too heavy for the moment.

profiles.notes: Run all five elements in order; skipping participants or principles is the most common failure mode.

bestWhen: A chronic interpersonal or process challenge needs fresh angles
Group has appetite for play, embodiment, and a bit of awkwardness
avoidWhen: Group is risk-averse, low-trust, or hierarchically tense
Topic is too sensitive or personal for embodied role-play

pairsWellAfter.reason: Loosens the group and surfaces concrete situations that become improv scenes.
Nonverbal warm-up that lowers self-consciousness before stepping into role-play.
Practiced helping moves give players a vocabulary they can replay on stage.

pairsWellBefore.reason: Each person names a concrete behavior change inspired by what worked on stage.
Group debrief turning the scenes into shared insights and behavioral commitments.
Codify the behaviors that the best scenes revealed into hard team rules.

useInstead.reason: Nonverbal exploration of the same chronic challenge without performance exposure.
Conversational peer help on the same chronic challenge instead of role-play.
Structured peer consultation when embodied prototyping is too vulnerable for the group.

profiles.notes: Test psychological safety first; if leaders won't play, players won't either, and the format collapses.
